text: This is the case of a fifty-four-year-old male who was submitted to a rectal resection with Hartmann's procedure for a rectal tumor eight months before. He presents with a rectal stem fistula at the time of a Hartmann's reversal.

The surgeon performs a simultaneous trans-anal transabdominal approach. Trans-anal arthroscopy reveals a suspicious fistula tract at the five o'clock position. The fistula is closed transanally using non-absorbable double figure-of-eight sutures, with no evident injury to the pelvic cavity observed during transabdominal inspection.

The surgeon perforates the anterior surface of the rectal stump using a mono-curved coagulation hook inserted transanally. A rendezvous approach is adopted, temporarily inserting a small silicone tube into the abdomen. The coagulation hook is replaced with straight grasping forceps, and the silicone tube is passed through the rectal stump out of the anus. The spike of a circular stapler is connected to the tube, and the stapler is introduced into the anus. The tube is removed, and the spike is connected to the anvil, creating the anastomosis.

The surgeon inspects the repaired fistula located inferior to the anastomosis, which shows no defects. Contrast-enhanced ICG perfusion is applied to assess the anastomosis, positioned five centimeters from the pectineal line, before concluding the procedure with a protective ileostomy.

During the transanal revisional surgery, the surgeon identifies and removes previous intraluminal sutures from the posterior segment of the anastomosis, where a suspected fistula was noted in a post-operative enema.
The surgeon cleans the defect using a coagulating hook, brushing the area to prepare for repair. Sutures are introduced into the D-port, and a new repair is performed on the rectal stem fistula.

Colonoscopy at four months showed a sinus fistula tract, which was not confirmed by NMR. After two months, the protective idea of STEMI is reversed, and at six months, the patient is doing well with the regular transit.
